title: OOP Fundamentals: Classes, Objects, Instances
obj: Students will be able to define a class, instantiate objects from it, and distinguish between a class and its instances.
content: a class defines what kind of object you can make — attributes (data) and methods (behavior)
a specific object created from a class; each instance has its own attribute values
`class Dog:` — then methods start with `def name(self, ...):`
called automatically on `Dog('Rex')`; usually assigns initial attribute values
refers to the specific instance inside a method — how methods read/write that instance's data
`class Dog:` is the Dog blueprint; `rex = Dog('Rex')` is one real dog
assess: Write a `Student` class with name and grade attributes and a `.promote()` method that increments grade
Create 3 instances of `Student` and verify each has independent state
Short answer: why does `self` appear as the first parameter of every method?

title: OOP Methods and Combining Classes
obj: Students will be able to declare methods on a class, call them on instances, and combine classes via composition or inheritance.
content: def inside class; takes `self`; can read/write self's attributes; returns a value or None
most methods act on instance data; static methods don't need self and belong to the class
an object contains other objects (a Car HAS-A Engine); wire via attributes
class Dog(Animal): — Dog inherits methods from Animal and can override them
favor composition for loose coupling; use inheritance only for clear IS-A relationships
assess: Given an `Animal` class with a `.speak()` method, write a `Dog` subclass that overrides `.speak()`
Lab: build a `Classroom` that contains a list of `Student` objects and a `.class_average()` method
Short answer: why do most style guides say 'prefer composition over inheritance'?

title: Python Operators: Logical, Relational, Boolean, Math
obj: Students will be able to apply the full operator set (arithmetic, comparison, logical, bitwise) and predict the result of compound expressions.
content: + - * / // % **; precedence; integer vs float division; PEMDAS applies
== != < > <= >= — return True/False; can chain like `0 < x < 10`
and, or, not; short-circuit evaluation — `a and b` skips b if a is False
&, |, ^, <<, >> — operate on binary representations; used in flags and low-level code
`not a == b` parses as `not (a == b)`; parenthesize when in doubt
assess: Predict the output of 5 expressions mixing arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators
Lab: rewrite a multi-line if chain using compound boolean expressions
Short answer: what does short-circuit evaluation mean, and why is it useful?

title: Python Functions, Parameters, and Return Values
obj: Students will be able to define Python functions with positional, keyword, and default parameters, return values, and reason about scope.
content: `def greet(name):` — name is a parameter; the body runs on call
`return x` sends a value back; without return, the function returns None
variables inside a function are local; read-only access to outer scope; global keyword exists but avoid
first line of function body can document purpose/args/returns
assess: Write a function `average(nums)` with a docstring, handle empty list case, test on 3 inputs
Debug: given a function that modifies a global variable, refactor to use parameters + return
Short answer: what's the difference between positional and keyword arguments?

title: Using External Python Libraries
obj: Students will be able to install, import, and use external Python libraries common in data science and AI.
content: `import math`, `import numpy as np`, `from pandas import DataFrame` — different access styles
pip install name; virtual environments keep projects isolated; pip freeze > requirements.txt
check the official docs first; scan the quickstart; then find specific functions
module not installed? typo in name? Python version mismatch? case-sensitive?
assess: Lab: install numpy, import as np, and compute the mean of [1,2,3,4,5]
Read: browse the Pandas DataFrame docs and summarize 3 commonly-used methods
Troubleshoot: given an ImportError, list 3 most likely causes

title: Choosing Python Data Structures: List, Set, Dict, Tuple
obj: Students will be able to select the appropriate Python data structure for a given task and reason about trade-offs.
content: ordered, mutable, allows duplicates — the default when order matters
unordered, unique elements, fast membership test — use to dedupe or test 'is X in this group'
key-value, unordered (insertion-order preserved in modern Python), fast lookup by key
ordered, immutable — use for fixed-size records or when you need a hashable collection
O(1) dict and set lookup; O(n) list `in`; choose based on the operation you do most
list of dicts, dict of lists — the patterns behind most real-world AI/DS data
assess: Given 4 scenarios (unique visitors, ordered events, lookup by id, fixed coordinate), pick the right structure
Lab: refactor a program that uses `if x in lst:` on a large list to use a set instead
Short answer: why is a dict's lookup faster than searching a list?

title: Strings and Text Processing
obj: Students will be able to construct, format, and transform strings using Python's string methods, important for NLP and data cleaning.
content: single/double/triple quotes; escape sequences; raw strings for regex and paths
`f'{name} scored {score:.1f}'` — modern Python formatting; supports expressions and format specs
lower, upper, strip, split, join, replace, find, startswith, endswith
s[0:5], s[-3:], s[::-1] reverses; produces new strings since strings are immutable
strings are Unicode; encoding matters when reading files (use encoding='utf-8')
assess: Lab: parse '2026-04-17' into year, month, day using string methods
Write: one-liner that splits a sentence into words, lowercases, and returns unique words
Short answer: why does 'abc'.replace('a','z') not modify the original string?

title: Professional Programming Practices
obj: Students will be able to apply professional practices: commenting, file organization, naming conventions, and version control basics.
content: comment the WHY, not the WHAT; no redundant comments; document tricky parts
src/ for code, tests/ for tests, data/ for data, one .py per class; __init__.py for packages
snake_case for functions/variables, PascalCase for classes, UPPER for constants
run a linter (ruff/black); follow PEP 8; 4-space indent; 79-100 char lines
git commit often, meaningful messages, .gitignore the secrets and venv; push to GitHub regularly
assess: Audit: review a given messy script and list 5 professional-practice violations
Lab: reorganize a single-file project into src/ + tests/ structure
Short answer: why is 'commit often' better than 'commit only when perfect'?
